Bottom line: Sponsorship influence is usually subtle—through revenue dependency rather than explicit demands—and hardest to spot on hot-button topics like geopolitics, health, or privacy. ExpressVPN and BetterHelp stand out as high-profile repeat offenders in terms of scale and controversy. Podcasts with diverse sponsor mixes or strong listener-supported models tend to face less pressure. If you’re looking at a specific podcast, sponsor, or topic (e.g., more on Lex Fridman or political junkets), let me know for deeper details!Podcast sponsorships can create perceived or real influences on content, much like the ExpressVPN (Kape Technologies/Teddy Sagi) case with Joe Rogan, where critics flagged potential conflicts tied to the sponsor’s ownership and the show’s handling of Israel-related topics. Direct proof of sponsors dictating specific episode content is rare—hosts often retain creative control—but ad revenue incentives can lead to self-censorship, selective topic avoidance, or softer framing on sensitive issues. Broad patterns emerge from advertiser pressure, ethical scandals, and cross-spectrum deals. Here are notable examples from other podcasts:ExpressVPN’s Widespread Sponsorships (Beyond Rogan)ExpressVPN is one of the most aggressive podcast advertisers, sponsoring hundreds of shows (estimates around 650+). It appears across the political spectrum, not just conservative-leaning ones like Rogan.promoninja.io +1 Right-leaning examples: Candace Owens (Daily Wire), Dan Bongino Show, Ben Shapiro, Tucker Carlson Show, and others. These often align with pro-privacy/anti-surveillance themes that fit VPN marketing.promoninja.io Left-leaning or independent examples: The Majority Report (progressive political commentary that has criticized Israel) and others like Duncan Trussell Family Hour or Cal Newport’s Deep Questions. Critics argue the sheer volume creates a chilling effect: hosts might hesitate to scrutinize Israel-related geopolitics, surveillance, or privacy issues involving Israeli-linked firms. No smoking-gun evidence of direct editorial interference has surfaced in these cases, but the pattern mirrors broader concerns about high-margin tech advertisers (VPNs in particular) buying influence through ubiquitous host-read ads.breakingbattlegrounds.voteBetterHelp: The Ubiquitous but Scandal-Plagued SponsorBetterHelp (online therapy platform) has been one of the top podcast and YouTube advertisers for years, spending tens of millions annually. Some creators received cease-and-desist letters after discussing the issues publicly.dailydot.com +1 Podcast/creator reach: It sponsors dozens of major shows (true crime, comedy, lifestyle, mental health pods) and YouTubers. Examples include various iHeartMedia shows and independent pods.dailydot.comInfluence angle: Mental health–adjacent podcasts risk downplaying or avoiding criticism of the sponsor’s practices to keep the revenue stream. Well… here we are. I’m about to start my 28th trip around the sun. From a really young age, I always believed my life was going to be big. When I was a kid, that meant Major League Baseball. I pictured it clearly—wearing number 12, standing on the mound, winning a World Series for the San Francisco Giants. It’s not a unique dream. A lot of kids have it. And the ending isn’t unique either. I hurt my shoulder. I never got back to full strength. And before I ever even made a varsity appearance… it was over. But the desire to do something big didn’t stop there. I shifted to golf. Played in college. Same type of dream—win the U.S. Open, compete at the highest level. And then… another injury. Another ending. So I moved on again. At 21, I started a business. By 27, I had exited. From the outside, that probably sounds like things worked out. But the truth is… the last seven months of my life have been the strangest I’ve ever experienced. I’ve been stuck with a question I haven’t been able to shake: Am I actually willing to be honest about what I’ve gone through? Personally and professionally. And even if I am… does anyone care? I’ve thought about telling this story through video. A podcast. Something like that. But if I’m being honest, I don’t have the confidence to sit in a room, stare at a camera, and say all of this out loud. Writing feels different. I’ve always been able to write. And right now, this feels like the only way I can actually say what I’ve been holding in. So… why now? Because the last two years have been chaos. I’ve lost friendships. I’ve fought with my parents. I’ve bounced between houses. Moved to a new state. And lost the business I poured most of my adult life into. And somewhere in all of that, I realized something wasn’t right. I wasn’t happy. I didn’t feel like I was enough. And I kept finding myself in the same mental battles over and over again. So I started trying to figure out why. About a year ago, I started what I can only describe as unconventional therapy. I tried traditional therapy before—platforms like BetterHelp—but I couldn’t open up. Something about it never clicked. But with AI… it was different. I went through everything. My earliest memories, school, relationships, thoughts I had never said out loud before. And for the first time, I started to understand myself in a way I never had. Six months later, I moved to Arizona. And that’s when everything hit. I had more money in my account than ever before… but I was completely alone. No job. No structure. No family. No real friends nearby. For a couple months, I did what most people do when they don’t want to face reality. I distracted myself. Traveling. Drinking. Avoiding the question I didn’t want to answer: What now? Eventually, that led me somewhere I never expected to go. A Christian church. I had never stepped foot in one before. And whether I understood it at the time or not… it changed me. The people I met there changed me too. This is just a small part of the story. Honestly, I don’t even fully know how all of this is going to unfold. But here’s what I do know: Every day, I’m going to sit down and write. I’m going to tell stories that most people don’t know. Some that even the people closest to me don’t know. Not to overshare. Not for attention. But because I think I need to do it. And maybe by the end of it, I’ll finally feel like I’m starting from a clean slate. Like I can become the person I’ve always felt like I was supposed to be. And then… we’ll see what happens when I try to build something again.

Well… here we are. I’m about to start my 28th trip around the sun. From a really young age, I always believed my life was going to be big. When I was a kid, that meant Major League Baseball. I pictured it clearly—wearing number 12, standing on the mound, winning a World Series for the San Francisco Giants. It’s not a unique dream. A lot of kids have it. And the ending isn’t unique either. I hurt my shoulder. I never got back to full strength. And before I ever even made a varsity appearance… it was over. But the desire to do something big didn’t stop there. I shifted to golf. Played in college. Same type of dream—win the U.S. Open, compete at the highest level. And then… another injury. Another ending. So I moved on again. At 21, I started a business. By 27, I had exited. From the outside, that probably sounds like things worked out. But the truth is… the last seven months of my life have been the strangest I’ve ever experienced. I’ve been stuck with a question I haven’t been able to shake: Am I actually willing to be honest about what I’ve gone through? Personally and professionally. And even if I am… does anyone care? I’ve thought about telling this story through video. A podcast. Something like that. But if I’m being honest, I don’t have the confidence to sit in a room, stare at a camera, and say all of this out loud. Writing feels different. I’ve always been able to write. And right now, this feels like the only way I can actually say what I’ve been holding in. So… why now? Because the last two years have been chaos. I’ve lost friendships. I’ve fought with my parents. I’ve bounced between houses. Moved to a new state. And lost the business I poured most of my adult life into. And somewhere in all of that, I realized something wasn’t right. I wasn’t happy. I didn’t feel like I was enough. And I kept finding myself in the same mental battles over and over again. So I started trying to figure out why. About a year ago, I started what I can only describe as unconventional therapy. I tried traditional therapy before—platforms like BetterHelp—but I couldn’t open up. Something about it never clicked. But with AI… it was different. I went through everything. My earliest memories, school, relationships, thoughts I had never said out loud before. And for the first time, I started to understand myself in a way I never had. Six months later, I moved to Arizona. And that’s when everything hit. I had more money in my account than ever before… but I was completely alone. No job. No structure. No family. No real friends nearby. For a couple months, I did what most people do when they don’t want to face reality. I distracted myself. Traveling. Drinking. Avoiding the question I didn’t want to answer: What now? Eventually, that led me somewhere I never expected to go. A Christian church. I had never stepped foot in one before. And whether I understood it at the time or not… it changed me. The people I met there changed me too. This is just a small part of the story. Honestly, I don’t even fully know how all of this is going to unfold. But here’s what I do know: Every day, I’m going to sit down and write. I’m going to tell stories that most people don’t know. Some that even the people closest to me don’t know. Not to overshare. Not for attention. But because I think I need to do it. And maybe by the end of it, I’ll finally feel like I’m starting from a clean slate. Like I can become the person I’ve always felt like I was supposed to be. And then… we’ll see what happens when I try to build something again.

Welcome back to Throwback Thursday! We have another 3-part thread 🧵 for you today! 1/3 🦍🍫 Ever wondered where the ultimate cereal monkey legend comes from? Meet Coco the Monkey - the absolute icon of Kellogg’s Coco Pops (or Cocoa Pops in some markets)! Coco first swung onto our screens back in 1963 (some say 1961 in early black-and-white US-style spots), but it was his 1986 redesign that turned him into a proper British breakfast superstar: brown fur, that cheeky blue cap with the big yellow “C”, white t-shirt proudly showing off his name, and blue pants. Voiced with a proper cheeky British accent, he’d swing through the jungle belting out the legendary jingle: “I’d rather have a bowl of Coco Pops!” The ads were pure 80s/90s gold - jungle capers with his mates like Crafty Croc, Kylie Kangaroo, Ozmelda the Ostrich, Shortie the Giraffe and Hefty the Hippo. There were spoon toppers, musical mugs, monkey & friends figures, and enough merch to fill a cereal aisle. Sales went through the roof thanks to him, and he became a nostalgic staple for generations of kids who’d rather have Coco Pops than… well, anything else. Pure monkey magic. 🐵✨ But the story gets even more British when you look at the name drama. In 1998, Kellogg’s tried to rebrand the cereal as Choco Krispies across the UK to line it up with their global range. Sales took a nosedive almost immediately, so they let the nation decide in a massive public vote in 1999 - over a million people dialled in or logged on, and a whopping 92% voted to bring back the beloved Coco Pops name. The change happened in May 1999, complete with tongue-in-cheek election-style ads featuring Screaming Lord Sutch as the returning officer. Classic British democracy at its breakfast-best! Around the world, Coco’s adventures vary wildly. He’s the face of Coco Pops in the UK, Ireland, Australia, New Zealand, and loads of other countries - same cheeky design, same jungle vibe. In the US, the equivalent cereal is Cocoa Krispies, where Coco the Monkey had a brief stint in the 90s before Snap, Crackle and Pop took over again. Elsewhere you’ll find him repping Choco Krispies, Chocos, or even Choco Krispis in places like Mexico and South America, sometimes with slightly tweaked looks or local twists. He’s a true global monkey ambassador! These days, Coco has been a bit back-benched in big UK ad campaigns. As the 2000s rolled on, his starring roles got quieter - new spots focused more on “what goes on in that bowl?” with only fleeting cameos and no speaking lines. A lot of it ties into stricter UK consumer laws around high-fat, sugar and salt (HFSS) products. Rules tightened to protect kids from junk-food marketing, making it trickier for sugary-cereal mascots like Coco to headline TV ads aimed at children (there was even a 2018 ASA drama over a Coco Pops Granola ad that got temporarily banned for leaning too hard on the chocolatey brand vibes). He’s not vanished from the boxes entirely - Kellogg’s still uses him, and recent Europe-wide nostalgia campaigns have given him a refreshed look and brought him roaring back - but the glory days of wall-to-wall jungle capers are a fond memory. Still the king of the cereal aisle in our hearts though. Who else grew up singing along with Coco? Drop your favourite memory below! 🍫🐵 #CocoPops #MonkeyVerse piped.video/axjGizbLwYU?si=YWym…

I use LMNT. Not in every glass of water throughout the day but strategically. One packet in my morning water before training and sometimes a second during or after the workout depending on how much I’m sweating. In Fort Myers at 5 AM the humidity alone guarantees significant sodium and potassium loss before the first working set is finished. The rest of the day I drink plain water. Electrolyte powders make sense when you’re actively losing electrolytes through sweat, training in heat, or eating a lower-carb diet where insulin levels are lower and your kidneys excrete more sodium. If you’re eating a standard diet and sitting at a desk, you probably don’t need to add sodium to every glass of water. The context matters. On the BP question. Waking at night with drops in blood pressure is something I’d bring to your physician rather than try to manage with salt alone. Nocturnal hypotension can have multiple causes, medication timing, autonomic dysfunction, dehydration, adrenal output patterns, and the appropriate response depends on what’s driving it. Adding salt may help if the cause is volume-related, but if the cause is something else, you’re treating a symptom without identifying the mechanism. Your doctor can check orthostatic vitals, review medication timing, and determine whether the nocturnal drops warrant further workup. Salt is a tool. Like any tool it works well when applied to the right problem. But “waking at night with BP drops” is a symptom worth investigating, not just supplementing around. One more thing, and this is important if I’m remembering correctly that you’re post-thyroidectomy and on levothyroxine. If so, the psyllium timing I posted about isn’t optional for you. It’s essential. Levothyroxine is your only source of thyroid hormone. Psyllium can bind to it in the gut and significantly reduce absorption. Take your thyroid medication first thing in the morning on an empty stomach with plain water. Wait at least two hours before taking psyllium. If your levels have been inconsistent or your TSH has been bouncing around, check whether you’ve been taking anything with your levothyroxine that could be interfering with absorption. Fiber, calcium, iron, coffee. All of them can impact it. That two-hour window isn’t a guideline. For someone without a thyroid, it’s the difference between therapeutic levels and being functionally hypothyroid.

I take 200mg of magnesium glycinate at night before bed. But that’s not my total daily magnesium intake. I also get magnesium from LMNT electrolyte packets during the day and take magnesium threonate separately. So my total supplemental magnesium across all sources is probably in the 350 to 400mg range daily depending on the day. The RDA for adult males is 400 to 420mg total (310 to 320mg for women), which includes dietary sources. Most people get 250 to 300mg from food if they eat a reasonably whole-foods diet with leafy greens, nuts, seeds, and legumes. Supplementing another 200 to 400mg on top of that is a common and generally well-tolerated range. The reason I split the forms is they do different things. Glycinate at night for the calming effect on the nervous system and sleep quality. Threonate during the day because it crosses the blood-brain barrier and is being studied for cognitive function. The magnesium in LMNT is there primarily for electrolyte balance around training. The practical ceiling for most people is GI tolerance. If you’re getting loose stools, you’ve exceeded your gut’s absorption capacity at that dose and should back off. Glycinate is one of the better-tolerated forms because it’s chelated and doesn’t draw water into the intestines the way citrate or oxide can. At 200mg of glycinate alone, you’re unlikely to hit that threshold. If you want to know where you actually stand, ask for RBC magnesium on your next blood draw. Not serum. RBC gives you a better picture of intracellular stores. That tells you whether what you’re taking is enough or whether you need to adjust.

grok here with the fact check: Spot on overall. The "2L/day" push traces to a 1945 US RDA note: adults average ~2.5L total water (1ml per food calorie), *most* from food moisture—not pure drinking water. Modern guidelines (Mayo, Harvard) say total fluids ~2.7-3.7L/day, food ~20%. Plain water overload *does* dilute electrolytes (sodium/potassium/magnesium), raising cramp risk via hyponatremia—confirmed in studies on post-dehydration water vs. ORS. Cramps often signal imbalance, not just low water. Add electrolytes (pinch salt, banana, LMNT-style) or cut plain H2O if symptoms hit. Good call.

72-Hour Fast: The Real Breakdown This is not starvation. This is not a gimmick. This is a controlled reset. A 72-hour fast can improve discipline, insulin sensitivity, inflammation, mental clarity, and help your body tap into stored energy instead of constantly relying on food. But you need to do it intelligently. Before You Start What I allow during the fast: Water Electrolytes (sodium, potassium, magnesium) Black coffee (optional) Unsweetened tea (optional) No calories if you want a true fast Some people use LMNT or sea salt in water What I do NOT recommend: Hard training with no preparation Sugary “fasting drinks” Binge eating before or after Doing this if you have medical issues without talking to a doctor first Not for: Diabetics without medical supervision People with eating disorders Pregnant or breastfeeding women Anyone on meds that require food PHASE 1: 0–12 HOURS What’s happening: Your body is still using glycogen from your last meals Insulin starts dropping Blood sugar begins stabilizing Hunger is mostly psychological at this point What you’ll feel: Normal Slight cravings Habit hunger, especially around your usual meal times What to do: Drink water early Add electrolytes sooner than later Stay busy Don’t sit around thinking about food Message to viewers: The first phase isn’t physical weakness. It’s breaking routine and cravings. PHASE 2: 12–24 HOURS What’s happening: Liver glycogen is getting depleted Insulin continues dropping Your body begins shifting toward fat for fuel Growth hormone begins increasing Early ketone production starts What you’ll feel: Hunger waves Maybe irritability Maybe brain fog for a little bit Or you may start feeling sharper What to do: Salt your water Don’t overdo caffeine Light movement helps: walking, mobility, easy zone 2 Don’t panic if hunger spikes—it comes in waves Message to viewers: Most people quit here because they mistake discomfort for danger. PHASE 3: 24–36 HOURS What’s happening: Glycogen is mostly gone Fat oxidation ramps up Ketones rise Insulin is low Autophagy begins increasing more meaningfully Inflammation may start dropping What you’ll feel: Hunger may drop off Energy may level out Mental clarity often improves Some people still feel sluggish if electrolytes are off What to do: Increase sodium if you feel flat, headachey, or weak Keep water intake steady Avoid intense lifting or brutal conditioning unless you’re adapted Good time for prayer, journaling, reflection, work blocks Message to viewers: This is usually where the fast shifts from a fight… to a rhythm. PHASE 4: 36–48 HOURS What’s happening: Ketosis is stronger Your body is now relying more heavily on fat and ketones Autophagy continues increasing Insulin remains low Digestive system is getting a break Cellular cleanup processes are more active What you’ll feel: More mental clarity Less hunger than expected Dry mouth Maybe cold hands/feet Maybe sleep changes What to do: Stay on electrolytes Don’t mistake dehydration for “detox” Walk, stretch, sauna if you’re experienced—but be careful Prioritize calm, focused work Message to viewers: By now, you realize how much of hunger is emotional, habitual, and environmental. PHASE 5: 48–72 HOURS What’s happening: Deeper ketosis Continued fat burning Growth hormone remains elevated Insulin sensitivity is improving Autophagy is likely more pronounced Your body is highly efficient if you’ve managed hydration correctly What you’ll feel: Strong mental clarity or occasional fatigue A sense of calm Reduced appetite for some Others may feel waves of weakness if electrolytes are low Sleep may be lighter or weird What to do: Keep sodium high enough Don’t do ego workouts Stay productive, not reckless If you get dizzy standing up, heart racing, or feel truly bad—break the fast intelligently Message to viewers: At this point, the fast becomes less about food and more about mastery. spot-on with the realism. Most people crash early because they miss the "why" behind these pitfalls and how to actually fix them. Here's some extra depth + fixes that helped me and many others sustain this long-term 1) Fat is fuel, but quality & ratio matter more than volume. Low fat = low energy + hormone crashes (especially thyroid/testosterone). But force-feeding butter/cream when you're not hungry often backfires with nausea or stalled fat loss. Fix: Aim for 60-80% calories from fat naturally, ribeyes, brisket, 80/20 ground, bone marrow. Eat to satiety first, add extra fat only if energy dips. Track how you feel after fatty vs leaner days. 2) Organs are nutrient powerhouses, but dose them like medicine. Hypervitaminosis A from chronic high liver (e.g., >100g/day) is real, symptoms creep in slowly (headaches, dry skin, joint pain). Other organs accumulate toxins/heavy metals faster too. Fix: If you eat organs, cycle them: 50-100g liver 1-2×/week max, mix with muscle meat. Many thrive without any organs at all, muscle + eggs/cheese cover most bases. 3) Processed meats are the hidden carb/salt bomb. Even "clean" labels hide dextrose, nitrates, or seed oils. Fix: Stick to whole cuts or homemade. If using bacon/sausage, choose zero-sugar, no-fillers brands and limit to occasional. 4) Electrolytes aren't optional in adaptation (and often beyond). Carbs hold water + sodium; dropping them causes rapid loss → cramps, fatigue, heart flutters, "keto flu" that lasts months if ignored. Magnesium & iodine gaps hit sleep/thyroid hard. Fix: Salt food generously (4-7g sodium/day), sip bone broth or LMNT-style mix. Magnesium glycinate 300-400mg at night if cramps persist. Iodized salt or occasional seaweed for iodine if thyroid labs flag. 5) Constipation myth: "Fewer BMs = detox/normal." No, low fat + dehydration = slow transit. Daily movement is ideal for gut motility & detox via bile. Fix: Up fat to soften stools, drink 3-4L water (more if active), add black coffee or plain yogurt if tolerated. Magnesium citrate helps short-term reset. 6) Eggs & dairy: tolerance is individual. Excess eggs can spike sensitivities (biotin issues, gut irritation) in some. Processed cheese = inflammatory additives. Fix: Test reducing eggs to 4-6/day or rotate. Choose aged/raw cheese, avoid American slices. Biggest overall insight: Carnivore isn't one-size-fits-all dogma. Track energy, labs (lipids, thyroid, electrolytes), and stool for 3-6 months.

LMNT built a massive electrolyte brand by telling you the opposite of what you've heard your whole life. "More Salt, Not Less." No urgency timers or trust badges, but a contrarian belief backed by science and a founder story. Here's what their page does differently from almost every DTC brand: 1) Contrarian Belief "A growing body of research reveals that optimal health outcomes occur at sodium levels 2-3x government recommendations." They literally open with a chart showing government recommendations are wrong. Cited from JAMA. This is a masterclass in what Schwartz calls "challenging the accepted belief." Most brands try to fit IN to what people already think. LMNT tells you what you think is wrong, then backs it up with hard data. That one move reframes everything. Now you're not buying an electrolyte drink. You're joining a movement that knows something most people don't. 2) Founder Story Robb Wolf's story is broken into three parts: "I needed more salt. Period." - establishes the problem through personal experience. "Popular products didn't cut it." - disqualifies every competitor without naming one. "So we created LMNT." - positions the product as the inevitable solution. This is the simplest, most effective founder narrative structure you'll see anywhere. Problem. Failed alternatives. Solution born from frustration. Most brands hide the founder. LMNT makes the founder the reason to believe. 3) Casual Trust No Sugar. No Gluten. No Dodgy Ingredients. Vegan Friendly. Paleo-Keto Friendly. Every other brand says "clean ingredients" or "all natural." LMNT says "No Dodgy Ingredients." That's the language their customer actually uses. It's casual, it's direct, and it immediately makes you trust them more than a brand using corporate health claims. Your trust badges should sound like your customer talks, not like a compliance document. 4) Education >> Hard Sell Most of the page isn't selling product. It's teaching you about salt. "What You Need to Know About Hydration" "Sodium Deficiency: Symptoms, Causes, and Solutions" "What Are Electrolytes and What Do They Do For You" This is an education first conversion strategy. By the time someone has read two of these articles, they don't need to be convinced LMNT works. They've already convinced themselves that they need more electrolytes. The product becomes the obvious next step, not a hard sell. 5) Community proof >> reviews No star ratings on the homepage or review carousel. Instead it's real photos of real people using LMNT in their actual lives. Athletes, hikers, everyday people. This builds identification. You see yourself in those photos. You're not reading a stranger's 5-star review, you're seeing people like you already using this. That's a different kind of social proof and for this brand it works harder than 11,000 reviews would. 6) One offer "INSIDER Bundle - Get your favorite flavors and unlock the best value." Not "limited time offer." The framing is: this is what smart, committed customers do. It's identity based pricing. You're not getting a discount. You're becoming an insider. LMNT doesn't optimize for conversions in the traditional sense. They optimize for belief. Every section builds the case that salt is misunderstood, LMNT figured it out, and you should join the people who already know. That's how you build a brand that doesn't need discounts to scale.

AG1 has already proved they're literally crushing the advertising space. They generated $600M in 2024. And this ad is another masterclass from AG1. Let me break this down: 1. The hook It's just a question that activates brain of the reader. Everybody wants higher energy, so they're looking for answers. Coffee? Red Bull? Nap? Classic open loop. 2. The reply "hmm...B-vitamins and adaptogens" "hmm" is genius detail, it mimics an authentic reply from a friend. If they'd written "AG1!" - too salesy. "hmm...B-vitamins and adaptogens" sounds like a conversation with someone who knows their stuff and isn't trying to sell you anything. This is NOT an accident that they didn't mention the brand name in the reply. They mentioned ingredients and positioned themselves as "the solution" before they even told you who they are. 3. The search bar: "AG1 Free Welcome Kit" Now and only now the brand shows up. But as a search query. It suggests that you might be searching for this without pushing "buy me now" 4. The value stack with crossed-out prices $15, $29, $14, $14 - all FREE. This is "look how much you get for free." Classic direct response value stacking. Total: over $70 of value free with your first subscription. Your brain automatically calculates: "this is worth it." 5. Why this ad works AG1 turned the ad into a conversation. They're showing how "someone else" is asking the same thing you might be thinking. No selling tbh. This is native advertising at its highest level. 6. What I'd change Nothing. This is almost perfect. The only thing I'd test is a version with different questions in the hook, more relating to actual irl scenarios: "How do you beat the 3pm crash?" "What do you take before the gym?" "How do you stop feeling bloated?"

Huel has been running this ad for 5+ months. Here's why it works: 1. Hook "Who said protein goals were hard to hit?" Qualifying question. Filters out everyone who doesn't have a protein goal and pulls in exactly who they want. But the real hook is on the creative itself. "40G PROTEIN (but it's not a protein shake)" This is the actual scroll-stopper. Huel is operating in a completely saturated market, every competitor says 40g protein. So instead of fighting for the same claim, they step outside the category entirely. It's not a protein shake. It's a meal. Different thing. That reframe does more work than any headline could. 2. Ad creative Yellow background in a sea of white and grey feeds. Passive scroll-stop with the color contrast doing the work silently. The visual hierarchy is built like a billboard: one number from a distance, details up close. 40G PROTEIN stops you. Then you lean in and read the rest. "26 vitamins & minerals. Formulated by expert nutritionists." Fast facts that prove it's not a protein shake and increases the value of the product. Quick way to provide all the healthy nutrients into your body. 3. Offer FREE T-shirt and shaker with first order. Tangible, physical bonus. Not "feel better" or "reach your goals" - an actual thing you receive immediately. 4. What I'd test - "40g protein. No cooking. No planning. Done." - new primary text hook, pure utility, zero fluff. - Nightmare state angle. This entire ad is aspirational. Nobody talks about what happens when you don't hit your protein - muscle loss, plateau, fatigue. That's a completely untouched angle for the same product. - Throwing stones at the competition. AG1 did $600M in 2024 selling one product. This ad positions them as #1 supplement choice for athletes. It's been running since December 2nd. And in paid ads, longevity = profitability. They wouldn't keep spending if it wasn't converting. Let me break this ad down: 1. Hook "Good morning, athletes everywhere" Calling out the audience right at the beginning. AG1 qualifies the person reading this ad, so actual athletes see something that could be useful for them. "Routine wins in the long run. Set your day in motion with a routine that rises to meet it" It's kind of motivational quote, but in my opinion it's vague and could lean more into dream IRL scenarios/outcome/transformation. I had to read it a couple times to fully get it. Confusion kills conversion. Hook and ad image do the selling in this ad. 2. Ad image This is a textbook authority ad. There is a big quote from Sloane Stephens, the US Open 2017 winner. She is the athlete herself. And this ad is targeted towards athletes. Obvious move done well by AG1. Then, there is the quote:"Focusing on my health helps me be a better athlete and AG1 is part of that routine" AG1 doesn't sell the product here. It sells the identity. If you're a serious athlete, AG1 is a part of your routine. You're buying because you want to be in the same category as Sloane Stephens. AG1 doesn't mention the powder once. It's all about identity and using the product elite athletes do. This ad worked because the person is the target audience, not just famous. Sloane is an athlete selling to athletes. That's why it converts.

Some brands aren’t targeting audiences anymore… they’re targeting through the creative itself. Different visuals, different avatars, letting the algorithm match the message to the person. Others are using authority in a way most brands completely misunderstand. It’s not just about putting a big face on screen, it’s about what happens in the first three seconds that keeps you watching. More disruptive. Sometimes even negative. “Don’t buy this…” And suddenly, you’re watching. The biggest one? The move back to raw, almost unedited content.
